testingFrequency: Annually on the neighborhood testing schedule
dueBasis: Marana says required backflow assemblies must be tested annually by a certified tester registered with Marana Water. Failure to complete the test can lead to disconnection of potable water until a passing test is completed, and failed tests must be repaired or replaced within 7 days.
coveredPropertyTypes: Homes with more than one source of water including CMID areas
officialListLabel: Open the Marana approved backflow tester list
penalties: Marana warns that failure to complete the annual test can lead to disconnection of potable water until the device is compliant. Failed tests must be repaired or replaced within 7 days, and replacement permits must be submitted 24 hours before installation.
sourceExcerpt: Marana is one of the strongest Arizona utility pages because it publishes a real approved tester list, annual schedule rules, 7-day failed-test repair timing, and potable-water disconnection consequences.
verdictSummary: Marana is a strong Arizona utility because it combines a public approved tester list, annual scheduling, 7-day repair timing, and potable-water shutoff risk.
whoIsAffected: Marana commercial, industrial, multifamily, high-rise, and special residential customers, especially those in areas with non-potable irrigation water from CMID.
residentialNotes: Marana is unusually useful for residential pages because the town explicitly names neighborhoods with non-potable irrigation water that must protect the potable system.
Potable reconnection can require a passing test from a Marana-approved tester.
commercialNotes: Commercial and multifamily intent is strong because Marana publishes tester registration, fees, SAMS workflow, and failed-test timing.
This is a high-quality utility page for annual-testing and failed-test lead intent.

annualTesting.summary: Marana requires annual testing by a certified tester that is registered with Marana Water and uses neighborhood schedule letters plus SAMS for reporting.
highlights: Failure to test can lead to potable-water disconnection.
workflowSteps: Wait for the Marana letter or schedule window.
Use a tester on the approved Marana list.
Make sure the result is posted through the accepted Marana workflow.

irrigation.summary: Marana is especially good for irrigation because non-potable irrigation neighborhoods like Gladden Farms and Rancho Marana are part of the public explanation.
highlights: CMID-related irrigation is a named local hazard.
Permits and inspections matter when assemblies are replaced or reconnected.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the property is tied to CMID or another auxiliary source.
Use a Marana-approved tester for the annual test.
If the device fails, repair or replace it within 7 days and keep the permit timing intact.

fireLine.summary: Marana is less fire-line-specific than Tempe or Buckeye, but the same annual approved-tester and 7-day failed-test rules still govern larger protected services.
highlights: High-rise and multifamily properties are named in the public page.
Replacement requires permit timing and inspection discipline.
workflowSteps: Confirm the protected-service class and permit needs.
If the device fails, complete repair or replacement inside the 7-day window.
Wait for the Marana testing letter or schedule period.
Hire an approved tester from the Marana list.
Complete the annual test and post the result.
If the device fails, repair or replace it within 7 days so potable service is not disconnected.
failureHighlights: Marana publishes one of the strongest Arizona failed-test timelines.
Potable-water disconnect language makes the risk concrete.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is market-priced, but Marana adds tester registration, schedule discipline, and shutoff risk.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest costs rise quickly because failed assemblies must be fixed or replaced within 7 days.
costBand.pricingNotes: The strongest local cost driver is avoiding potable-water disconnect and repeat permit/inspection work.
